text: The Vipassana teacher S.N. Goenka described the forgetting of meditation instructions mid-session as 'sankharic pull' — the accumulated momentum of habitual mental patterns dragging awareness toward pleasant or aversive objects. In his tradition, sati is explicitly trained as a counter-force to this pull: the meditator repeatedly redirects attention to the breath or body sensations not because wandering is shameful, but because each redirection weakens the groove. The metaphor is hydraulic: habitual channels run deep, but new channels can be cut. Sati is the act of cutting — not once, at the beginning of practice, but ten thousand times across a lifetime.
question: In Goenka's teaching, why does the meditator repeatedly redirect attention rather than achieving a single permanent redirection?

options.text: Because the breath changes from moment to moment and requires constant fresh observation
Because sati is not yet strong enough in beginning practitioners to hold focus without reinforcement
Because each redirection weakens the habitual groove, and cutting new channels requires repeated effort
Because sankharic pull is an external force that sati can only temporarily suppress
